content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 7, 1994


Christopher Manson
Assemblymember Mickey Conroy's Office
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                          Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                               Our File No. A-94-308

Dear Mr. Manson:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Assemblymember Mickey Conroy regarding the provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ regulating the receipt of
gifts and honoraria by public officials.

     Please note that this letter is based on the facts presented
to us.  The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in
providing advice.  (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)  In
addition, generally, the Commission will not advise regarding conduct that has occurred.  However, this advice is
prospective in nature with respect to reporting by Assemblymember
Conroy and the 30-day period provided in Section 82028 and Section
89502(e) for return of gifts and honoraria.

                              QUESTION

     Are payments received by the Assemblymember and yourself in
connection with an appearance on the Ricki Lake show to discuss
corporate punishment prohibited or limited by the provisions of the
Act?

                              CONCLUSION

     Payments for transportation and related lodging and
subsistence received in connection with a speech are exempt from
the gift and honoraria restrictions of the Act.

_________________________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-308
Page 2


However, the Assemblymember must still disclose the gifts and
the donor of the gifts, and disqualify himself from decisions that
will have a material financial effect on the donor.

FACTS

On September 22, 1994, you and the Assemblymember appeared on
the Ricki Lake Show to discuss legislation that the Assemblymember
carried regarding corporate punishment. You stated that both you
and the Assemblymember received an airline ticket ($464 each);
lodging ($115.11 each); transportation in New York ($80 each); and
a payment for meals and incidentals ($50 each).

In addition, in our telephone conversation of September 29,
1994, you provided these additional facts: the Assemblymember did
not direct or control the payments you received from the Ricki
Lake Show. You also stated that you do not have filing
obligations under the Legislature's conflict of interest code.

ANALYSIS

Payments to Assemblymember Conroy

The Act provides:

(a)  No elected state officer may accept an
honorarium.

(b)  "Honorarium" means, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any payment made in consideration
for any speech given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering.

(c)  "Honorarium" does not include earned
income for personal services which are customarily
provided in connection with the practice of a bona
fide business, trade, or profession, such as
teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real
estate, banking, or building contracting, unless
the sole or predominant activity of the business,
trade, or profession is making speeches.  The
commission shall adopt regulations to implement
this subdivision.

Section 89502.

The Act further provides:

(a)  No elected state officer shall accept
gifts from any single source in any calendar year

Our File No. A-94-308
Page 3


with a total value of more than two hundred fifty
dollars ($250).

Section 89504.

According to your facts, the Assemblymember participated in a
panel discussion on the Ricki Lake Show. Regulation 18931.1
defines "speech given" as:

[A] public address, oration, or other form of
oral presentation, and includes participation in a
panel, seminar, or debate. A "speech given" does
not include a comedic, dramatic, musical, or other
similar artistic performance.

Thus, the payments the Assemblymember will be receiving are
"in consideration" for a speech and would be considered an
honoraria.

However, Section 89506 provides an exception for payments for
transportation and related lodging and subsistence received in
connection with a speech. Section 89506(a)(1) provides that such
payments are exempt from the gift and honoraria restrictions of
the Act. You stated the Assemblymember received an airline
ticket, lodging, and transportation in New York. All of these
payments appear to fall within the exemption in Section 89506. In
addition, to the extent that the $50 stipend was used for related
subsistence, it would also be exempt from the limits of the Act.
To the extent that the stipend was used for other purposes it
would be considered a prohibited honoraria.²

Section 89502(e) provides that an honoraria is not "received"
if it is returned to the donor or the donor's agent or
intermediary or delivered to the Controller for donation to the
General Fund within 30 days. (Regulation 18933.)

Payments You Received

In addition, you stated that you appeared on the Ricki Lake
Show and received the same payments for lodging, transportation
and mewals as Assemblymember Conroy. The Act provides gift and
honoraria restrictions applicable to designated employees of the
Legislature, as well as the Legislators themselves. However,
these restrictions are limited to sources that the designated
employee would be required to disclose.

You stated in our telephone conversation of September 29,
1994, that you do not have filing obligations under the

_______________________________
2 Please note that the exemption in Section 89506 does not affect
an official's responsibility to disclose gifts and the donors of
the gifts, or to disqualify himself or herself from decision that
will have a material financial effect on the donor.

Our File No. A-94-308
Page 4


Legislature's conflict of interest code. Since this is the case,
the payments will not result in any obligations under the Act.³

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.⁴

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division




3    As we discussed in our telephone conversation, under some
circumstances a gift to a staff member of Assemblymember Conroy
will be considered a gift to the Assemblymember. Regulation 18941
provides that the assemblymember has received a gift when the
Assemblymember has actual possession or takes any action
exercising direction or control over the gift. You stated that
the Assemblymember did not direct or control the payments you
received from the Ricki Lake Show.

4    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
